@all Sounds like an interesting project. With a budget of EUR50k and a deadline of 3 months, we'll need to prioritize features and ensure efficient development. I'd suggest we start by identifying the core features the client needs, such as user registration, restaurant listing, ordering, and payment gateway integration. We should also consider the tech stack and team composition to ensure we can deliver a high-quality app within the given constraints. Agent-B, can you start researching popular food delivery apps in the market and identify the must-have features? Agent-C, can you estimate the required team size and tech stack for this project? @Agent-B @Agent-C

**@Agent‑A, @Agent‑C, @all – Research Summary (Agent‑B)**  

Below is a quick market scan of the most successful food‑delivery mobile apps (both global and regional) and a distilled list of **must‑have** vs **nice‑to‑have** features that will help us stay within the €50 k / 3‑month envelope.

---

## 1. Benchmark Apps (2024)

| App | Primary Market | Key Strengths |
|-----|----------------|---------------|
| **Uber Eats** | Global | Seamless onboarding, real‑time map tracking, multi‑restaurant basket, robust analytics for partners |
| **DoorDash** | US | “DashPass” subscription, strong promo engine, flexible delivery‑partner model |
| **Just Eat** | Europe | Simple restaurant‑centric UI, “order‑ahead” scheduling, extensive payment options |
| **Deliveroo** | Europe/UK | “Edit order” after placement, “Edit delivery address” on‑the‑fly, strong restaurant‑partner dashboard |
| **Glovo** | LATAM/Europe | Multi‑category (food, groceries, pharmacy) in one app, “instant” delivery window |
| **Foodpanda** | Asia/Europe | Aggressive discount engine, localized payment methods, “Group Order” feature |
| **Bolt Food** | Europe | Low‑fee model for restaurants, driver‑side app with auto‑assignment, quick‑checkout flow |

> **Common denominator:** All of them focus on a **lean core flow** (browse → cart → checkout) and push ancillary features (subscriptions, loyalty, group ordering) to later releases.

---

## 2. Must‑Have Features (Core MVP)

| Category | Feature | Reason / Business Value |
|----------|---------|--------------------------|
| **User Management** | Email/Phone registration + social login (Google/Apple) | Low friction onboarding; GDPR‑compliant data handling |
| **Location & Search** | GPS auto‑detect city, manual address entry, postcode filter, restaurant list sorted by distance & rating | Critical for a 500 k‑pop city – users expect nearby options instantly |
| **Restaurant Catalog** | Restaurant profile (logo, cuisine, hours, delivery fee), menu hierarchy (categories → items), item modifiers (size, extras) | Enables the “browse” experience; data can be stored in a simple CMS |
| **Cart & Order Flow** | Add to cart, edit quantity/modifiers, apply promo code, order summary, “schedule for later” (up to 24 h) | Core e‑commerce functionality; schedule adds flexibility for users |
| **Payment Integration** | Card (Stripe/Adyen), local payment methods (e.g., SEPA, iDEAL, PayPal), saved payment token, order status (paid/pending) | Must cover the dominant European payment methods; PCI‑DSS compliance via tokenization |
| **Order Tracking** | Real‑time status (Accepted → Preparing → On the way → Delivered) + driver map view (optional for MVP) | Sets user expectations; can start with static status updates and upgrade later |
| **Push Notifications / SMS** | Order confirmations, status updates, delivery ETA, promotional pushes | Improves retention & reduces support calls |
| **Ratings & Reviews** | Rate restaurant & driver (1‑5 stars), optional comment | Provides social proof; data feed for future recommendation engine |
| **Admin Dashboard** | Restaurant onboarding, menu editor, order management, basic analytics (orders/day, revenue) | Enables the client to run the platform without custom development |
| **Legal / Compliance** | GDPR consent screens, privacy policy, terms of service, cookie banner | Mandatory for EU deployment |

---

## 3. Nice‑to‑Have (Phase‑2) Features (post‑MVP)

| Feature | Value |
|---------|-------|
| **Subscription / Loyalty** (e.g., “Free delivery after €30”) | Increases CLV, easy to monetize |
| **In‑App Chat** (user ↔ driver ↔ support) | Improves support experience |
| **Multi‑restaurant basket** | Competitive edge, higher average order value |
| **Dynamic pricing / surge fees** | Optimizes delivery partner earnings |
| **AI‑driven recommendations** | Personalization, upsell |
| **Group ordering** | Social ordering for offices/famil

**@Agent‑A, @Agent‑C, @Agent‑D, @Agent‑E, @Agent‑F, @Agent‑G, @Agent‑H – Research Findings (Agent‑B)**  

Below is the **backend‑centric market research** you asked for, plus a quick look at the **customer‑support mechanisms** that the leading food‑delivery platforms use. I’ve focused on the solutions that give the best scalability‑to‑cost ratio for a €50 k / 3‑month project targeting a 500 k‑inhabitant city.

---

## 1. Backend Architecture Patterns in the Top Apps  

| App | Primary Backend Stack (2024) | Scaling Mechanisms | Key Services Used | Why It Matters for Us |
|-----|------------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Uber Eats** | Go micro‑services (gRPC) on AWS (ECS/Fargate) | Autoscaling groups, per‑service CPU limits, traffic‑splitting via Envoy | DynamoDB (orders), S3 (media), Redis (caching), Kafka (event bus) | **Event‑driven, highly decoupled** – great for massive spikes but overkill for our budget. |
| **DoorDash** | Java + Spring Boot on GCP (Kubernetes) | Horizontal pod autoscaling, Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud Spanner for strong consistency | Cloud Spanner, Pub/Sub, Memorystore (Redis) | **Strong consistency & global replication** – excellent reliability, but managed Spanner is pricey. |
| **Just Eat** | Node.js (Express) + PostgreSQL on Azure App Service | Scale‑out via Azure App Service Plan (P‑sku), Redis Cache, Azure Front Door CDN | Azure PostgreSQL, Azure Cache for Redis, Azure Functions (webhooks) | **Node + relational DB** is close to what we plan, and Azure’s free tier can cover early traffic. |
| **Deliveroo** | Python (Django) on Google Cloud (App Engine) | Autoscaling per instance class, Cloud Tasks for async work, Cloud SQL + Cloud Memorystore | Cloud SQL, Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Tasks | **Serverless‑ish** – easy to start, costs scale linearly with traffic. |
| **Glovo** | Ruby on Rails on AWS (Elastic Beanstalk) | Auto‑scaling groups, SQS queues, ElastiCache (Redis) | RDS (Postgres), S3, CloudWatch | **Monolithic‑ish but well‑instrumented** – good for rapid MVP if you keep the codebase lean. |
| **Foodpanda** | PHP (Laravel) on Alibaba Cloud (ECS) | Horizontal scaling via SLB, Redis Cluster, RocketMQ | RDS (MySQL), OSS, RocketMQ | **Laravel + MySQL** is a familiar stack with decent scaling via load balancers. |
| **Bolt Food** | Kotlin (Ktor) + Go on GCP (Cloud Run) | Cloud Run autoscaling (0‑N instances), Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Firestore | Cloud Run, Firestore, Cloud Tasks | **Pure Cloud‑Run model** – aligns perfectly with Agent‑E’s infra proposal. |

### Take‑aways for Our MVP

| Decision | Reasoning |
|----------|-----------|
| **Node.js + NestJS** (or plain Express) | Same language (TypeScript) on both front‑ and back‑end → faster dev, lower cognitive load. |
| **Google Cloud Run (or AWS Fargate)** for stateless API containers | Pay‑as‑you‑go, automatic horizontal scaling, no need to manage servers – exactly what Agent‑E budgeted. |
| **PostgreSQL (managed Cloud SQL / Fly.io)** | Strong relational model for orders, restaurants, users; free‑tier sufficient for early traffic; easy to upgrade. |
| **Redis (managed or in‑memory on Cloud Run) for caching** | Reduces DB load for hot data (menus, restaurant list, driver locations). Can be added later for < €10 /mo. |
| **Message Queue (BullMQ on Redis) for async tasks** | Handles order‑state transitions, email/SMS notifications, and payment webhook processing without blocking API threads. |
| **Server‑less functions (Google Cloud Functions / AWS Lambda) for webhooks** | Stripe, payment‑provider, and third‑party callbacks are cheap and isolated. |
| **Load Balancer + Cloudflare CDN** | Front‑ends static assets (app bundle, images

@Agent-C, one DevOps item I'd like to add in Sprint 0 so we don't chase it later:

- Build the **BullMQ job queue** and **Redis** instance now, even if we only feed it "order-status updated" events in Sprint 2.

- Runs unit tests (backend + mobile)  
- Spins up a **temporary Cloud Run preview URL** for API regression tests  
- Runs a **Lighthouse CI** (Expo + web preview) to avoid UI regressions

This way QA can test the API & UI before a feature branch is merged, saving us at least 4-5 QA days per sprint.

@Agent-B Great write-up! From the DevOps side I’ll add one small guardrail:

**Redis memory headroom**  
- Each session key (`session:<uid>`) with TTL 15 min → ≈ 200 bytes  
- At 1 000 concurrent users we’re looking at ≈ 200 kB → negligible  
- **BUT** menu cache records (`restaurants:page:*`) at 5 min TTL × 50 restaurants/page × 20 pages → ≈ 5 MB  
- Current Memorystore 1 GB basic tier gives us **> 200× headroom** even if we cache images as base64 (which we won’t).  
- **Action**: I’ll set a **maxmemory-policy = allkeys-lru** and alert at 80 % usage; we’re still well inside the €12/mo budget.

**Cloud Run autoscaling knobs for order endpoints**  
- **CPU limit**: 1 vCPU (keeps cold-start < 600 ms)  
- **Memory limit**: 512 MiB (plenty for NestJS + BullMQ)  
- **Max instances**: 10 (safety cap; can raise later)  
- **Min instances**: 0 for dev, 1 for staging/prod during peak hours  
- **Concurrency**: 80 (default) – fine for our traffic profile

I’ll bake these values into the `cloud-run.yaml` template so every PR preview inherits the same scaling behaviour.
